food_subgroup: Baked pastry with spiced potato curry filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Curry Puff. Nearest parent is a baked filled pastry or hand pie. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry products — filled baked pastry with spiced vegetable filling (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard baked potato curry puff weighs approximately 90g and consists of a flaky, multilayered baked wheat pastry shell enclosing a spiced potato filling with diced onions, curry powder, and sometimes hard-boiled egg or chicken pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Puff (baked, potato) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database; no exact code found; values adapted from HPB baked pastry and spiced potato filling composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact baked curry puff entry found in HPB Foodo. Values adapted from HPB pastry and spiced potato data. Old Chang Kee-style spiral pastry curry puff reference used as benchmark.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — wheat flour pastry base; GI elevated by refined flour shell; potato filling adds moderate GI starch.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 340mg per piece (90g) — about 15% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; relatively moderate sodium for a savory snack.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension may consume a single curry puff occasionally; the sodium content is moderate, but the high saturated fat and calorie density make it a poor choice for frequent snacking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat pastry has a high GI (~65); per-piece carbohydrate load is approximately 30g; high fat content from butter/margarine slows but does not prevent significant postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should limit curry puff consumption to occasional treats; the refined flour pastry shell and potato filling together produce a moderate-high glycaemic response.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from butter or margarine in the pastry; refined flour has a pro-inflammatory glycaemic impact; curry spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric) provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit curry puff consumption due to the high refined flour and saturated fat content; the anti-inflammatory spices in the filling provide minimal offsetting benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Singapore Curry Puff is one of the most beloved local snacks, consisting of a flaky, multi-layered baked wheat flour pastry shell (made with butter or margarine for its characteristic spiral layers) filled with a mildly spiced potato curry seasoned with turmeric, curry powder, onions, and sometimes diced chicken or hard-boiled egg. The baked version is distinguished from the fried epok-epok by its golden, oven-baked shell. It is a staple of traditional bakeries, kiosks (Old Chang Kee is the iconic chain), and hawker stall breakfast menus across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a curry puff
baked vs fried curry puff which is healthier
can a diabetic eat curry puff
Old Chang Kee curry puff calories

confidence_reason: No exact baked curry puff entry found in HPB Foodo. Values adapted from HPB pastry and potato data. Confidence medium.
notes: Baked version has ~20% fewer calories than the deep-fried version. Versions with egg or chicken will have higher protein. Vegetarian in standard potato filling without meat; contains egg and dairy (butter) so not vegan. Trans fat content depends on whether margarine with partially hydrogenated fat is used.
